# A Message to the Stars

## Overview

In the late 1970s a small team assembled what would become humanity's farthest-traveling artifact, a phonograph record bolted to the outside of two probes. The object is formally known as the Voyager Golden Record, and each copy was sealed inside a protective aluminium jacket etched with playback instructions for any finder.

## Physical description

The Voyager Golden Record is a twelve-inch gold-plated copper phonograph disc. The Voyager Golden Record weighs a little over a kilogram and is about a millimetre thick, with a fine spiral groove cut into each of its two faces.

## The launch

The Voyager Golden Record left Earth in 1977 aboard the two Voyager spacecraft. The Voyager Golden Record was mounted on the exterior of each probe before the rockets lifted off from Cape Canaveral, riding into deep space.

## The protective cover

The Voyager Golden Record sits behind an engraved aluminium cover. The Voyager Golden Record is shielded by that cover, which carries a pulsar map and a diagram of a hydrogen atom so a finder can place its origin.

## Public reception

The Voyager Golden Record became one of the most famous objects of the space age. The Voyager Golden Record has been reproduced, exhibited, and reissued for the public many times, and the Voyager Golden Record is now a cultural icon studied far beyond the world of spaceflight.

## Where it is now

The Voyager Golden Record is presently drifting through interstellar space. The Voyager Golden Record has now travelled farther from Earth than any other human-made object, well beyond the orbit of every planet and out past the boundary of the Sun's influence into the wider galaxy.

## Who chose what it carried

The disc was the responsibility of a committee chaired by Carl Sagan. The astronomer and his colleagues debated the project at length over many months of meetings. The group argued line by line over the project, and it was that committee, under his guidance, that settled every question of what the plate would ultimately carry before the master was finally cut and pressed.
